title: Te Arotake Taiao o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku / The Mangakōtukutuku Stream Environmental Assessment
subject: Mathematics
culturalContext: Students will investigate the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, a significant stream that flows through our rohe and holds cultural importance to Ngāti Kahungunu. The stream's name references the native shrub (kōtukutuku) that traditionally grew along its banks, and its waters have sustained our community for generations. Students will apply mathematical reasoning to assess water quality and ecosystem health, connecting to the concept of kaitiakitanga - our responsibility as guardians of this taonga.
learningObjectives: Calculate and interpret water quality data (pH, turbidity, temperature) using appropriate mathematical operations and units of measurement
Create and analyze data visualizations (tables, graphs, charts) to identify patterns and trends in stream health indicators
Apply proportional reasoning to calculate pollution concentrations and assess environmental impact levels
Communicate mathematical findings about the stream's health using appropriate terminology and cultural context

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga ki Te Awa - Connecting to the Stream
activities.description: Students visit Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with kaumātua to hear pūrākau (traditional stories) about the stream's significance and learn proper protocols for engaging with the water. They make initial observations and develop inquiry questions about the stream's health.

activities.title: Pūtaiao me te Pāngarau - Science and Mathematics Integration
activities.description: Students collect water samples and use testing kits to measure pH levels, turbidity, and temperature. They record data in organized tables, convert between units, and calculate averages for multiple sampling points along the stream.

activities.title: Tātari Raraunga - Data Analysis Workshop
activities.description: Using collected data, students create line graphs showing changes in water quality parameters over time and bar graphs comparing different sampling locations. They analyze trends and identify mathematical patterns in the environmental data.

activities.title: Te Hītori o Te Awa - Historical Comparison
activities.description: Students compare current water quality data with historical records from regional council archives, calculating percentage changes in pollution levels and using proportional reasoning to understand environmental changes over the past 20 years.

activities.title: Te Whakatau Kaitiaki - Guardian Decision Making
activities.description: Working in groups, students use their mathematical findings to develop evidence-based recommendations for stream restoration. They create mathematical models showing potential improvements and present their kaitiakitanga action plans to the class.

resources: Hawke's Bay Regional Council water quality monitoring kits and historical data sets
Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua and environmental kaitiaki as cultural advisors
NZ Maths digital tools for data visualization and analysis
Local stream restoration case studies from Hawke's Bay Tomorrow environmental initiative

tasks: Create a comprehensive data report with tables and graphs showing water quality measurements
Write an analysis explaining mathematical patterns and trends in the stream health data
Develop a kaitiakitanga action plan with mathematical justification for proposed restoration strategies
Present findings to peers using appropriate mathematical language and cultural context

nzcAlignment: Mathematics and Statistics: Statistical investigation - conducting surveys, experiments, and observational studies
Mathematics and Statistics: Number and algebra - patterns and relationships, equations and expressions
Mathematics and Statistics: Geometry and measurement - units, measuring instruments, and precision
Science: Nature of science - investigating in science, communicating in science
Social Sciences: Place and environment - how people interact with natural and cultural environments
